title: Entanglement Dynamics of Separable Squeezed States in Finite Memory Structured Reservoir
abstract: Entanglement in continuous-variable Gaussian systems is a key resource, and common reservoirs can both suppress and generate correlations. Existing work focused on pre-entangled states or Markovian baths, leaving open whether separable squeezed inputs entangle in structured environments or under modulation. We study two bosonic modes coupled to a common reservoir, each initialized in a separable squeezed vacuum. Dynamics are analyzed utilizing Gaussian covariance methods, evolved under approximate Non-Markovian quantum state diffusion (QSD), finite-temperature pseudomode embeddings, and Bures-based non-Markovian diagnostics. We identify three mechanisms absent in Markovian dynamics: (1) A detuning condition that freezes entanglement trajectories across reservoir correlation times; (2) birth, death, and revival of entanglement from orthogonal inputs; and (3) integer-locked beating with square-wave oscillations produced by periodic detuning. All mechanisms persist at finite temperature, with deviations bounded within 5% in cryogenic regimes and 20% at moderate occupations. These deviation bounds align with cryogenic cavity, phononic, and optomechanical platforms, where structured spectral densities and detuning modulation are already accessible. Structured reservoirs are shown to emerge as tunable entanglement resources for continuous-variable quantum technologies.
summary: This paper analyzes entanglement dynamics in bosonic systems coupled to structured, memory-retaining reservoirs. By employing advanced Gaussian methods, the authors reveal three unique non-Markovian phenomena—including entanglement revival and integer-locked beating—that are impossible in simple Markovian baths. These findings establish structured reservoirs as controllable resources for continuous-variable quantum technologies.
main_problem: The study investigates how entanglement dynamics evolve in continuous-variable Gaussian systems when coupled to structured, finite-memory quantum reservoirs, addressing gaps left by Markovian or pre-entangled state assumptions.
main_result: The authors identify three non-Markovian mechanisms—detuning-induced freezing, entanglement revival from separable inputs, and integer-locked beating—that are absent in Markovian dynamics.
method: The analysis employs Gaussian covariance methods, evolving the system dynamics using approximate Non-Markovian Quantum State Diffusion (QSD) and utilizing Bures distance diagnostics to quantify non-Markovianity.
model_or_system: The model consists of two bosonic modes coupled to a common bosonic reservoir, whose memory effects are modeled using an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck kernel. The analysis is extended to finite temperatures via pseudomode embeddings.
key_observables: Logarithmic negativity ($E_N$), Bures distance ($D_B$), and the non-Markovianity witness ($N$).
important_parameters: Detuning ($\delta_{AB}, \delta_{AE}$), reservoir correlation time ($\gamma$), thermal occupation ($ ar{n}$), and modulation amplitude ($\delta_0/\kappa$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximate QSD and pseudomode embeddings, though these methods successfully map the dynamics to maintain complete positivity.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the persistence of entanglement revivals and beating envelopes under structured coupling, and show the relative deviation of entanglement metrics across varying thermal occupations.
paper_structure: The paper builds from establishing the system Hamiltonian and initial states, developing the non-Markovian evolution equations (using QSD/pseudomodes), and then systematically analyzing the three key non-Markovian phenomena (freezing, revival, beating) under varying temperature and detuning conditions.
why_interesting: This work provides concrete, analytically derived mechanisms (like integer-locking and freezing laws) showing how structured environments can act as tunable resources for generating and controlling quantum entanglement, which is highly relevant for quantum optics and cavity QED.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Time evolution of the logarithmic negativity EN [Eq. 8] in a Markovian bath with mode A fixed at sa = +1 and mode B initialized at sb = −0.5 (blue), sb = −0.2 (orange), and sb = +0.5 (red). (b) Steady-state EN as a function of (sa, sb) over [−5, 5], with bright regions bounded by Simon criteria [Eqs. A13-A14].

FIG. 2: Steady-state logarithmic negativity EN [Eq. 8] versus thermal occupation ¯n in a Markovian bath. Results are shown for aligned (sa = sb = +0.5, orange), weakly orthogonal (sa = +0.5, sb = −0.2, red), and strongly orthogonal (sa = +0.5, sb = −0.5, blue) inputs. Aligned inputs retain finite entanglement up to ¯n≈1.5, weakly orthogonal inputs separate for ¯n ≈0.2, and strongly orthogonal inputs remain separable for all ¯n.

FIG. 3: (a) Time evolution of the logarithmic negativity EN [Eq. 8] in an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck bath for γ/κ = 0.5 (blue) and γ/κ = 5 (red) at fixed detuning δ∗ AE. Trajectories overlap within a few percent. (b) Relative deviation %EN = |∆EN/Ebaseline N | × 100% versus γ, referenced to the γ/κ = 0.5 curve. Deviations remain below 5%, concentrated near oscillation minima.

FIG. 4: Relative deviation %EN = |∆EN/Ebaseline N | × 100% of freezing dynamics versus thermal occupation ¯n. Deviations are bounded by ≤5% in the cryogenic regime (¯n ≤0.05) and ≤20% at moderate occupancies (¯n ≤0.2). Larger excursions occur only at short-time transients and late-time decay.

FIG. 5: (a) Time evolution of logarithmic negativity EN [Eq. 8] for orthogonal inputs (sa = +1, sb = −1) in structured baths with different detuning pairs (δAB, δAE)/κ, showing entanglement birth, death, and revival. (b) Markovian comparison, where no choice of δAE yields revivals and correlations decay rapidly. (c) Non-Markovianity measure N [Eq. 11] versus δAB/κ for the same conditions; in the large-δAE/κ regime the witness remains zero despite nontrivial EN(t).
